raw_response: {"rating": 5.5, "lean": "Right", "explanation": "This is an opinion piece that consistently advances right-leaning critiques of higher education, progressive academic culture, and urban crime policy. The author's own prose disparages courses associated with identity politics, prison abolition, and radical ethics while valorizing Western civilization and the classics. The density and consistency of these themes across the piece warrants a Right classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author uses mocking language in their own voice to describe courses associated with progressive values — calling them 'nonsensical progressive orthodoxy,' 'absurdity,' 'ridiculous,' and 'esoteric,' framing leftward academic trends as inherently illegitimate; (2) The author explicitly invokes the value of 'Western civilization' and the 'classics,' a recognizable right-leaning cultural framework, as the standard against which modern curricula should be measured; (3) The author frames shoplifting policy in New York as evidence of moral decay — 'Like our city's sense of right and wrong?' — editorializing against lenient criminal penalties, a position aligned with right-leaning law-and-order values; (4) Courses dealing with race, gender, sexuality, disability, queerness, and intersectionality are grouped together and dismissed in the author's own prose as lacking practical value outside campus, aligning with the right's rejection of identity politics and progressive academic frameworks; (5) The author invokes Gallup and Indeed polling data to validate skepticism of higher education, framing the data as 'damning,' lending rhetorical weight to a conservative populist critique of elite universities; (6) The reference to Karl Marx being assigned more than Shakespeare or Plato is presented as self-evidently troubling, reflecting a right-leaning concern about leftist ideological capture of academia without offering any counterbalancing perspective."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is an opinion piece that consistently advances right-leaning critiques of higher education, progressive academic culture, and urban crime policy. The author's own prose disparages courses associated with identity politics, prison abolition, and radical ethics while valorizing Western civilization and the classics. The density and consistency of these themes across the piece warrants a Right classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author uses mocking language in their own voice to describe courses associated with progressive values — calling them 'nonsensical progressive orthodoxy,' 'absurdity,' 'ridiculous,' and 'esoteric,' framing leftward academic trends as inherently illegitimate; (2) The author explicitly invokes the value of 'Western civilization' and the 'classics,' a recognizable right-leaning cultural framework, as the standard against which modern curricula should be measured; (3) The author frames shoplifting policy in New York as evidence of moral decay — 'Like our city's sense of right and wrong?' — editorializing against lenient criminal penalties, a position aligned with right-leaning law-and-order values; (4) Courses dealing with race, gender, sexuality, disability, queerness, and intersectionality are grouped together and dismissed in the author's own prose as lacking practical value outside campus, aligning with the right's rejection of identity politics and progressive academic frameworks; (5) The author invokes Gallup and Indeed polling data to validate skepticism of higher education, framing the data as 'damning,' lending rhetorical weight to a conservative populist critique of elite universities; (6) The reference to Karl Marx being assigned more than Shakespeare or Plato is presented as self-evidently troubling, reflecting a right-leaning concern about leftist ideological capture of academia without offering any counterbalancing perspective.

explanation: This is an opinion piece that consistently advances right-leaning critiques of higher education, progressive academic culture, and urban crime policy. The author's own prose disparages courses associated with identity politics, prison abolition, and radical ethics while valorizing Western civilization and the classics. The density and consistency of these themes across the piece warrants a Right classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author uses mocking language in their own voice to describe courses associated with progressive values — calling them 'nonsensical progressive orthodoxy,' 'absurdity,' 'ridiculous,' and 'esoteric,' framing leftward academic trends as inherently illegitimate; (2) The author explicitly invokes the value of 'Western civilization' and the 'classics,' a recognizable right-leaning cultural framework, as the standard against which modern curricula should be measured; (3) The author frames shoplifting policy in New York as evidence of moral decay — 'Like our city's sense of right and wrong?' — editorializing against lenient criminal penalties, a position aligned with right-leaning law-and-order values; (4) Courses dealing with race, gender, sexuality, disability, queerness, and intersectionality are grouped together and dismissed in the author's own prose as lacking practical value outside campus, aligning with the right's rejection of identity politics and progressive academic frameworks; (5) The author invokes Gallup and Indeed polling data to validate skepticism of higher education, framing the data as 'damning,' lending rhetorical weight to a conservative populist critique of elite universities; (6) The reference to Karl Marx being assigned more than Shakespeare or Plato is presented as self-evidently troubling, reflecting a right-leaning concern about leftist ideological capture of academia without offering any counterbalancing perspective.
